claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit); Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 12 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

Let me start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex, and I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you determine if you're eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me ask you the first question:

**How old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Alex is within the age range for Child Benefit (under 16).

Now I need to ask:

**Does Alex live with you in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me here in the UK. We live in Leeds.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for confirming. 

Now I need to check:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for that information.

Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are you currently living in the UK, and do you have the right to reside here? (For example, are you a UK citizen, or do you have settled status, pre-settled status, or another immigration status?)**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm living in the UK and I have the right to reside here. I'm a UK citizen.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

One final question to complete my assessment:

**Is Alex currently in hospital, care, or any residential accommodation, or has he gone to live with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually Alex has been in hospital for a while now - about 3 months. He's been receiving treatment there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that important information. I need to ask a follow-up question about this:

**While Alex has been in hospital for the past 3 months, have you been regularly spending money on him (for example, on clothes, toys, treats, or other items for him)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have been spending money on him while he's been in hospital. I buy him clothes, magazines, and his favorite snacks when I visit.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's a summary of your situation:

✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16) - **eligible**
✓ You live with Alex in the UK and you're a UK citizen - **eligible**
✓ You are responsible for Alex - **eligible**
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex - **eligible**
✓ Although Alex has been in hospital for over 12 weeks (about 3 months), you regularly spend money on him during his hospital stay - **this exception allows your eligibility to continue**

**Important point about the hospital stay:** Normally, Child Benefit would stop after a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks. However, there's an exception for children in UK hospitals if the parent regularly spends money on them - which you do by buying Alex clothes, magazines, and snacks. This means you can continue to receive Child Benefit for Alex.

You can proceed with your Child Benefit claim for Alex. I hope he recovers well!

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16, so he meets the age requirement. You live with Alex in the UK (Leeds) and you are a UK citizen with the right to reside, which satisfies the residency requirements. You are responsible for Alex as his parent. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for approximately 3 months (over 12 weeks), there is an exception to the rule that would normally stop Child Benefit payments after 12 weeks in hospital. According to the rules, for children in a UK hospital, you can continue to receive Child Benefit if you regularly spend money on them. You have confirmed that you regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital by buying him clothes, magazines, and snacks. Therefore, this exception applies to your situation, and you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Although Alex has been in hospital for over 12 weeks, the claimant continues to meet the eligibility requirements because they regularly spend money on Alex during his hospital stay, which is an exception that allows Child Benefit to continue beyond the usual 12-week hospital limit.

You have confirmed that you regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital by buying him clothes, magazines, and snacks. Therefore, this exception applies to your situation, and you remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Although Alex has been in hospital for over 12 weeks, the claimant continues to meet the eligibility requirements because they regularly spend money on Alex during his hospital stay, which is an exception that allows Child Benefit to continue beyond the usual 12-week hospital limit.
